source_inspiration: IMO seed — permutations for young learners
stem_template: {name} has 3 coloured blocks: red, blue, and yellow. In how many different orders can {pronoun} line them up?

visual_manifest.art_brief: Three blocks labelled R (red), B (blue), and Y (yellow). Show one example arrangement: R-B-Y. An arrow suggests 'how many other ways?'
visual_manifest.alt_text: 3 coloured blocks that can be arranged.

misconceptions.feedback_child: 3 is how many blocks. Each ORDER is different! Try listing: RBY, RYB...
You found some but missed a few! Start with red first: how many ways?
Not 3×3. The first spot has 3 choices, next has 2, last has 1. 3×2×1 = ?

socratic_feedback.on_correct: 6 different orders! You discovered that 3×2×1 = 6. That's called a factorial!
socratic_feedback.generic_incorrect: Start with red first. How many ways can you arrange blue and yellow after it? Now try blue first...
socratic_feedback.encouragement_after_retry: List them out: red-blue-yellow, red-yellow-blue... keep going!

author: AP
